rows.v: Cognac region, France — double-distilled, oak aged
Dried fruit, spice, floral, honey, oak
Hennessy VS, Rémy Martin VSOP, Courvoisier
Dark chocolate, dried figs, aged cheeses
The most recognizable and widely available brandy — a great starting point
Gascony, France — single-distilled, more rustic than Cognac
Prune, dark chocolate, rustic spice, dried fruit
Roasted duck, prune desserts, strong cheeses
Often considered the "farmhouse" cousin of Cognac — bolder, more textured, and frequently better value for the age statement.
Peru or Chile — unaged grape brandy
Cocktails — its bright, clean profile is built for the Pisco Sour
Italy — distilled from grape pomace (skins, seeds, stems)
Earthy, herbal, raw fruit, sometimes floral
Espresso, biscotti, after a rich Italian meal
Traditionally a digestif — sharp and intense, often served in small pours after dinner rather than as a mixer.
Often served with a sugared rim — a great gateway into brandy cocktails for those used to sours.
Tastes like a boozy chocolate milkshake — order this as an after-dinner treat instead of dessert.
Pisco, lime juice, simple syrup, egg white
The egg white isn't optional here — it's essential to the signature frothy texture and a few drops of bitters on top.
Brandy, rye whiskey, sweet vermouth, Bénédictine, bitters
A New Orleans classic with five ingredients — order this if you want something layered and boozy, not a "first drink of the night."
Two ingredients, built over ice — one of the simplest "real" cocktails to order or make at home.
